concepts.title: Equation and Properties of rectangular hyperbola
concepts.conceptBody:
Rectangular or Equilateral Hyperbola 

A hyperbola is called rectangular if its asymptotes are at right angles. The asymptotes of $\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{{{a}^{2}}}-\frac{{{y}^{2}}}{{{b}^{2}}}=1$ are $y=\pm \left( b/a \right)x$ so they are perpendicular if $-{{b}^{2}}/{{a}^{2}}=-1$ i.e.,${{b}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}$, i.e., $a=b$.

Hence equation of a rectangular hyperbola can be written as ${{x}^{2}}-{{y}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}$

(i) ${{\text{a}}^{2}}={{\text{a}}^{2}}\left( {{\text{e}}^{2}}-1 \right)$ gives ${{\text{e}}^{2}}=2$ i.e. $\text{e}=\sqrt{2}$.

(ii) Asymptotes are $\text{y}=\pm \text{x}$

(iii) Rotating the axes by an angle $-\pi /4$ about the same origin, equation of the rectangular hyperbola ${{x}^{2}}-{{y}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}$ is reduced to $xy={{a}^{2}}/2$ or $xy={{c}^{2}},\left( {{c}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}/2 \right)$. In $xy={{c}^{2}}$, asymptotes are coordinate axes.

(iv) Rectangular hyperbola is also called equilateral hyperbola.

Rectangular Hyperbola referred to its asymptotes as axis of coordinates: 

(i) Equation is $xy={{c}^{2}}$ with parametric representation $x=ct,y=\frac{c}{t},t\in R\sim \left( 0 \right)$.

(ii) Equation of chord joining the points $P\left( {{t}_{1}} \right)\And \left( Q\left( {{t}_{2}} \right),x+{{t}_{1}}{{t}_{2}}y=c\left( {{t}_{1}}{{t}_{2}} \right) \right.$

(iii) Equation of the tangent at $\text{P}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ is $\frac{\text{x}}{{{\text{x}}_{1}}}+\frac{\text{y}}{{{\text{y}}_{1}}}=2$ and at $\text{P}\left( \text{t} \right)$ is $\frac{\text{x}}{\text{t}}+\text{ty}=2\text{c}$.

(iv) Chord with a given middle point as ( $\text{h},\text{k})$ is $\text{kx}+\text{hy}=2\text{hk}$.

(v) Equation of the normal at $\text{P}\left( \text{t} \right)$ is $\text{x}{{\text{t}}^{3}}-\text{yt}=\text{c}\left( {{\text{t}}^{4}}-1 \right)$

(vi) Vertex of this hyperbola is $\left( c,c \right)$ and $\left( -c,-c \right)$; focus is $\left( \sqrt{2}c,\sqrt{2}c \right)$ and $\left( -\sqrt{2}c,-\sqrt{2}c \right)$, the directrices are $\text{x}+\text{y}=\pm \sqrt{2}\text{c}$ and $\text{l}($ L.R. $)=2\sqrt{2}\text{c}=$ T.A. $=$ C.A.

Q) If the normal at the point ' ${{\text{t}}_{1}}$ ' to the rectangular hyperbola $\text{xy}={{\text{c}}^{2}}$ meets it again at the point ' ${{\text{t}}_{2}}$ ', prove that ${{\text{t}}_{2}}=-1/{{\text{t}}_{1}}{{~}^{3}}$. 

Solution: 

The equation of the normal at $\left( \text{c}{{\text{t}}_{1}},\text{c}/{{\text{t}}_{1}} \right)$ to the hyperbola $\text{xy}={{\text{c}}^{2}}$ is $\text{xt}_{1}^{3}-\text{y}{{\text{t}}_{1}}-\text{ctt}_{1}^{4}+\text{c}=0$

If this passes through $\left( c{{t}_{2}},\frac{c}{{{t}_{2}}} \right)$, then

$\begin{array}{ll} c{{\text{t}}_{2}}\text{t}_{1}^{3}-\frac{\text{c}}{{{\text{t}}_{2}}}{{\text{t}}_{1}}-\text{ct}_{1}^{4}+\text{c}=0 \Rightarrow \text{t}_{2}^{2}\text{t}_{1}^{3}-{{\text{t}}_{1}}-\text{t}_{1}^{4}{{\text{t}}_{2}}+{{\text{t}}_{2}}=0 \\ \Rightarrow \text{ t}_{1}^{3}{{\text{t}}_{2}}\left( {{\text{t}}_{2}}-{{\text{t}}_{1}} \right)+\left( {{\text{t}}_{2}}-{{\text{t}}_{1}} \right)=0\Rightarrow {} \left( \text{t}_{1}^{3}{{\text{t}}_{2}}+1 \right)\left( {{\text{t}}_{2}}-{{\text{t}}_{1}} \right)=0 \\ \Rightarrow \text{ t}_{1}^{3}{{\text{t}}_{2}}+1=0 {} \left[ \therefore {{\text{t}}_{2}}\ne {{\text{t}}_{1}} \right] \\ \Rightarrow {{\text{t}}_{2}}=-1/\text{t}_{1}^{3} {} {} \\ \end{array}$

Q) If a circle cuts the rectangular hyperbola $xy=1$ in the points $\left( {{x}_{r}},{{y}_{r}} \right);r=1,2,3$, 4. Prove that ${{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{x}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}_{3}}{{\text{x}}_{4}}={{\text{y}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{2}}{{\text{y}}_{3}}{{\text{y}}_{4}}=1$ 

Solution: 

Let the equation of the circle be

${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}+2gx+2fy+k=0$

The equation of the hyperbola is $\text{xy}={{\text{c}}^{2}}$

Eliminating y from these two equations, we get

${{x}^{2}}+\frac{1}{{{x}^{2}}}+2gx+2f\frac{1}{x}+k=0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{x}^{4}}+2g{{x}^{3}}+k{{x}^{2}}+2fx+1=0$

This is a fourth degree equation in $\text{x}$ giving four values of $\text{x}$, say ${{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{x}}_{2}},{{\text{x}}_{3}}$ and ${{\text{x}}_{4}}$.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{x}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}_{3}}{{\text{x}}_{4}}=1$

Corresponding to every value of $\text{x}$, there is a value of $\text{y}$ given by $\text{xy}=1$.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{y}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{2}}{{\text{y}}_{3}}{{\text{y}}_{4}}=\frac{1}{{{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{x}}_{2}}{{\text{x}}_{3}}{{\text{x}}_{4}}}=1$

Q) A rectangular hyperbola whose center is $\text{C}$ is cut by any circle of radius $\text{r}$ in four points $P,Q,R$ and S. Prove that ${{C}^{2}}+{{C}^{2}}+{{C}^{2}}+{{C}^{2}}=4{{r}^{2}}$. 

Solution: 

Taking the rectangular asymptotes as the axes of reference the equation of the hyperbola and the

circle are

$x y=k^2$ …(1)

and ${{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{\text{y}}^{2}}+2\text{gx}+2\text{fy}+\text{c}=0$ …(2)

where $\mathrm{g}^2+\mathrm{f}^2-\mathrm{c}=\mathrm{r}^2$ (given) …(3)

Eliminating v between (1) and (2), we get a biquadratic in x giving us the abscissas of the four points of intersection as

${{\text{x}}^{4}}+2\text{gx}{{\text{x}}^{3}}+c{{\text{x}}^{2}}+2\text{f}{{\text{k}}^{2}}\text{x}+{{\text{k}}^{4}}=0$

Its roots are ${{x}_{1}},{{x}_{2}},{{x}_{3}}$ and ${{x}_{4}}$ then

$\begin{array}{l} {} & \sum {{\text{x}}_{1}}=-2\text{ g},\sum {{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{x}}_{2}}=\text{c} \\ \therefore  & \text{x}_{1}^{2}+\text{x}_{2}^{2}+\text{x}_{3}^{2}+\text{x}_{4}^{2}={{\left( \sum {{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)}^{2}}-2\sum {{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{x}}_{2}}=4{{\text{g}}^{2}}-2\text{c} \\ \end{array}$

Similarly, $\text{y}_{1}^{2}+\text{y}_{2}^{2}+\text{y}_{3}^{3}+\text{y}_{4}^{2}=4{{\text{f}}^{2}}-2\text{c}$

$\begin{array}{l} {} & \therefore \text{ C}{{\text{P}}^{2}}+\text{C}{{\text{Q}}^{2}}+\text{C}{{\text{R}}^{2}}+\text{C}{{\text{S}}^{2}} \\ {} & =\left( \text{x}_{1}^{2}+\text{y}_{1}^{2} \right)+\left( \text{x}_{2}^{2}+\text{y}_{2}^{2} \right)+\left( \text{x}_{3}^{3}+\text{y}_{3}^{2} \right)+\left( \text{x}_{4}^{2}+\text{y}_{4}^{2} \right) \\ {} & =\sum \text{x}_{1}^{2}+\sum \text{y}_{1}^{2}=4{{\text{g}}^{2}}-2\text{c}+4{{\text{f}}^{2}}-2\text{c} \\ {} & =4\left( {{\text{g}}^{2}}+{{\text{f}}^{2}}-\text{c} \right)=4{{\text{r}}^{2}} \\ \end{array}$

Q) If a circle cuts a rectangular hyperbola $xy={{c}^{2}}$ in $A,B,C$ and $D$ and the parameters of these four points be ${{t}_{1}},{{t}_{2}},{{t}_{3}}$ and ${{t}_{4}}$ respectively, prove the following : 

(a) ${{t}_{1}}{{t}_{2}}{{t}_{3}}{{t}_{4}}=1$, find the value of $\sum \frac{1}{{{t}_{1}}}$ 

(b) If $\text{H}$ be the orthocenter of the triangle $\text{ABC}$, then $\text{H}$ and $\text{D}$ are extremities of a diameter of the rectangular hyperbola. 

(c) The center of mean position of the four points bisects the distance between the centers of the two curves. 

(d) The center of the circle through $\text{A},\text{B}$ and $\text{C}$ is 

$\left[ \frac{c}{2}\left( {{t}_{1}}+{{t}_{2}}+{{t}_{3}}+\frac{1}{{{t}_{1}}{{t}_{2}}{{t}_{3}}} \right),\frac{c}{2}\left( \frac{1}{{{t}_{1}}}+\frac{1}{{{t}_{2}}}+\frac{1}{{{t}_{3}}}+{{t}_{1}}{{t}_{2}}{{t}_{3}} \right) \right]$ 

Solution: 

(a) Let the equation of the rectangular hyperbola referred to rectangular asymptotes as axes be $\text{xy}={{\text{c}}^{2}}$ or its parametric equations be $\text{x}=\text{ct}$ and $\text{y}=\frac{\text{c}}{\text{t}}$ …(1)

and that of the circle be

${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}+2gx+2fy+k=0$ …(2)

Solving (1) and (2), we get

${{c}^{2}}{{t}^{2}}+\frac{{{c}^{2}}}{{{t}^{2}}}+2gct+2f\cdot \frac{c}{t}+k=0$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{c}}^{2}}{{\text{t}}^{4}}+2\text{gct}{{\text{t}}^{3}}+\text{k}{{\text{t}}^{2}}+2\text{fct}+{{\text{c}}^{2}}=0$ …(3)

Above equation being of fourth degree in $t$ gives us the four parameters ${{t}_{1}},{{t}_{2}},{{t}_{3}}$ and ${{t}_{4}}$ of the

points of intersection.

$~\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{t}}_{1}}+{{\text{t}}_{2}}+{{\text{t}}_{3}}+{{\text{t}}_{4}}=\frac{2\text{gc}}{{{\text{c}}^{2}}}=-\frac{2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g}}{\text{c}}$ …(4)

$t_1 t_2 t_3+t_1 t_2 t_4+t_3 t_4 t_1+t_3 t_4 t_2=-\frac{2 f c}{c^2}=-\frac{2 f}{c}$ …(5)

$t_1 t_2 t_3 t_4-\frac{c^2}{c^2}-1$, which proves (a). …(6)

Dividing (5) by (6), we get

$\frac{1}{{{t}_{4}}}+\frac{1}{{{t}_{3}}}+\frac{1}{{{t}_{2}}}+\frac{1}{{{t}_{1}}}=-\frac{2f}{c}$

(b) Orthocenter of the triangle $\text{ABC}$ is

$\left( \frac{-c}{{{t}_{1}}{{t}_{2}}{{t}_{3}}},c{{t}_{1}}{{t}_{2}}{{t}_{3}} \right)$

Or $\text{H}$ is the point $\left( -\text{c}{{\text{t}}_{4}},\frac{-\text{c}}{{{\text{t}}_{4}}} \right)$,

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{t}}_{1}}{{\text{t}}_{2}}{{\text{t}}_{3}}{{\text{t}}_{4}}=1$, by $\left( 6 \right)$

and $\text{D}$ is the point $\left( \text{c}{{\text{t}}_{4}},\frac{\text{c}}{{{\text{t}}_{4}}} \right)$ and clearly $\text{H}$ and $\text{D}$ are extremities of a diameter.

(c) The center of mean position of the four points is

$\begin{array}{l} {} & \left[ \frac{\text{c}}{4}\left( {{\text{t}}_{1}}+{{\text{t}}_{2}}+{{\text{t}}_{3}}+{{\text{t}}_{4}} \right),\frac{\text{c}}{4}\left( \frac{1}{{{\text{t}}_{1}}}+\frac{1}{{{\text{t}}_{2}}}+\frac{1}{{{\text{t}}_{3}}}+\frac{1}{{{\text{t}}_{4}}} \right) \right] \\ {} & =\left[ \frac{\text{c}}{4}\left( \frac{-2\text{ g}}{\text{c}} \right),\frac{\text{c}}{4}\left( \frac{-2\text{f}}{\text{c}} \right) \right],\text{ from }\text{ (4) }\text{ and }\text{ (7) } \\ \end{array}$

or $\left( \frac{-g}{2},\frac{-f}{2} \right)$ which is clearly the mid-point of $\left( 0,0 \right)$ and $\left( -g,-f \right)$ i.e., of the join of the centers of the two curves.

(d) Let the circle through ABC meet the hyperbola in fourth point D; then from (4) and (7), the center $\left( -g,-f \right)$ is

$\left[ \frac{\text{c}}{2}\left( {{\text{t}}_{1}}+{{\text{t}}_{2}}+{{\text{t}}_{3}}+{{\text{t}}_{4}} \right),\frac{\text{c}}{2}\left( \frac{1}{{{\text{t}}_{1}}}+\frac{1}{{{\text{t}}_{2}}}+\frac{1}{{{\text{t}}_{3}}}+\frac{1}{{{\text{t}}_{4}}} \right) \right]$ …(8)

Also ${{\text{t}}_{1}}{{\text{t}}_{2}}{{\text{t}}_{3}}{{\text{t}}_{4}}=1$ from $\left( 6 \right)$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{t}}_{4}}=\frac{1}{{{\text{t}}_{1}}{{\text{t}}_{2}}{{\text{t}}_{3}}}$ …(9)

Putting the value of ${{t}_{4}}$ in (8), we get the required center in terms of the parameters of the given points $\text{A},\text{B},\text{C}$ in the form as given.
